network.overall_strategy: This network prioritizes the two literature-supported primary prebiotic sources of aspartic acid: (1) HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolysis, where aspartate is a major product, and (2) oxaloacetate-centered chemistry via Bucherer-Bergs and Cu2+/pyridoxamine transamination. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon hubs such as formate, acetate, pyruvate, glyoxylate, and oxaloacetate under alkaline vent conditions using FeS/Fe3S4/Fe3O4/Fe0 minerals. Surface environments concentrate HCN and NH3, enable cyanoacetylene and HCN oligomer chemistry, and support UV/mineral-assisted carbonyl and nitrile transformations. Biochemical/prebiotic settings then connect oxaloacetate, fumarate, and aspartate to broader protometabolic and peptide-forming chemistry. The network explicitly handles the oxaloacetate instability bottleneck by including the experimentally observed competing decarboxylation to pyruvate and by using rapid or branching OAA-consuming reactions that are documented in the literature.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed reduction of carbon dioxide to formate

conditions.pressure: alkaline vent to elevated hydrothermal pressure
conditions.other: alkaline vent-like pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization fluids

reactions.mechanism: Surface-mediated CO2 hydrogenation on Fe-S minerals generates reduced one-carbon species, with formate as an early fixed-carbon product.
reactions.reasoning: Alkaline vent models and mineral catalysis support Fe-S-mediated CO2 reduction to small organics; greigite and related Fe-S phases are central to primordial carbon fixation (Preiner et al., 2020; Santos-Carballal et al., 2018; Kitadai, 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fe0-mediated protometabolic formation of pyruvate and glyoxylate from CO2

conditions.other: anoxic CO2-rich system; hydrothermal protometabolic CO2 fixation window

reactions.mechanism: Reduced iron drives abiotic CO2 fixation through non-enzymatic network chemistry, yielding central keto-acid and aldehyde-acid intermediates including pyruvate and glyoxylate.
reactions.reasoning: Fe0-driven CO2 fixation produces central metabolic intermediates under hydrothermal conditions and is the strongest literature-supported upstream route feeding oxaloacetate chemistry; glyoxylate and pyruvate are established nodes in non-enzymatic protometabolic networks (Muchowska et al., 2019; Varma et al., 2018; Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2020).

reactions.name: Aldol-like coupling of pyruvate and glyoxylate to oxaloacetate precursor pool

conditions.other: CO2-rich anoxic aqueous conditions; oxaloacetate treated as transient product in protometabolic network

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate and glyoxylate undergo C-C bond-forming chemistry through hydroxy-keto intermediates that feed into oxaloacetate formation as a transient α-keto dicarboxylate node.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies glyoxylate-mediated access to oxaloacetate-equivalent chemistry as one of the best-supported prebiotic entries to OAA, though mechanistically via transient intermediates rather than a simple single-step biological analog; this reaction is a necessary network compression grounded in reported pyruvate-glyoxylate chemistry and flagged as a connected proxy for the Eschenmoser/Krishnamurthy-type route (Eschenmoser & Krishnamurthy, 2017; Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2020).

reactions.name: Cu2+-catalyzed transamination of oxaloacetate to aspartate

conditions.other: 5-10 mM reactants, 50 mM NH4HCO3 buffer, pH 6-10, reaction within ~1 hour to outrun OAA decarboxylation

reactions.mechanism: Metal-assisted pyridoxamine-mediated amino transfer converts the α-keto acid oxaloacetate into aspartate before extensive decarboxylation occurs.
reactions.reasoning: This is the best documented non-enzymatic transamination route to aspartate, giving ~5% yield within 1 hour; the literature also emphasizes that OAA decarboxylation competes strongly and that pyridoxamine availability remains unresolved, so the route is included with that caveat (Harrison et al., 2023; Mayer et al., 2021; Dherbassy et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of oxaloacetate to N-carbamoyl aspartate and dihydroorotate branch

conditions.other: neutral to slightly basic pH; cyanide plus ammonium bicarbonate equivalent chemistry in evaporitic/concentrating pools

reactions.mechanism: Oxaloacetate reacts with cyanide and ammonia/carbonate equivalents through Bucherer-Bergs chemistry to form N-carbamoyl aspartate, with branch access to dihydroorotate.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs pathway from α-keto acids is directly demonstrated for oxaloacetate and is one of the most important literature-prioritized routes because it links aspartate chemistry to pyrimidine synthesis; mineral surfaces are included here as concentrative agents to satisfy surface-network constraints, while the core transformation follows the documented aqueous chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl aspartate to aspartic acid

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolytic conversion following Bucherer-Bergs sequence

reactions.mechanism: Carbamoyl group hydrolysis releases free aspartic acid from N-carbamoyl aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: This step completes the experimentally demonstrated α-keto-acid Bucherer-Bergs route to aspartate and retains direct continuity with the reported chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Competing decarboxylation of oxaloacetate to pyruvate

conditions.other: half-life ~1-2 h at 25C near neutral pH; accelerated by divalent metals

reactions.mechanism: Spontaneous β-keto acid decarboxylation drains oxaloacetate to pyruvate, creating the major bottleneck in aspartate synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: The OAA decarboxylation race is the central challenge in prebiotic aspartate chemistry and must be explicitly included; this competing pathway explains why alanine often appears instead of aspartate in OAA amination experiments (Guzman & Martin, 2008; Barge et al., 2019; Harrison et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination network yielding alanine from pyruvate as OAA-loss product

conditions.other: 100C, anoxic; pyrite-forming reductive amination regime

reactions.mechanism: Iron-sulfur reductive amination converts pyruvate into alanine, illustrating the fate of oxaloacetate after decarboxylation.
reactions.reasoning: FeS/H2S reductive amination of α-oxo acids is experimentally documented, and alanine production from pyruvate is the key observed sink when OAA decarboxylates in iron-mediated systems; inclusion of this branch is required by the literature (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fe0-mediated CO2 reduction with hydroxylamine producing aspartate-containing product set

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal CO2 reduction with hydroxylamine as nitrogen source

reactions.mechanism: Reduced iron drives CO2 fixation into carboxylic acids and keto acids; in the presence of hydroxylamine, amino acid products including aspartate are formed within the broader protometabolic network.
reactions.reasoning: The literature specifically notes detection of aspartate in Fe0-mediated CO2 reduction experiments using hydroxylamine as nitrogen source under 30-100C and 35 bar, making this an important direct hydrothermal route to the target even if network intermediates are distributed across a product ensemble (Varma et al., 2018; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN and higher polymeric precursors

conditions.other: HCN concentration >10^-2 M at pH ~9.2; eutectic freezing can concentrate HCN to ~10 M near -23.4C

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes stepwise oligomerization through aminomalononitrile and diaminomaleonitrile toward higher oligomers that encode amino-acid precursors.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization is the most productive prebiotic route specifically for aspartate; DAMN is a key branch-point intermediate in the polymerization cascade and surface concentration/freezing are strongly supported by the literature (Ferris et al., 1974; Ferris et al., 1978; Matthews & Moser, 1966; Miyakawa, Cleaves & Miller, 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomers to glycine and aspartic acid

conditions.other: acid hydrolysis equivalent to 6N HCl, 110C, 24 h in analytical demonstration

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic breakdown of nitrogen-rich HCN oligomers releases amino acids, with aspartate and glycine among the major products.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomers is the strongest literature-supported route to aspartate as a major product rather than a trace contaminant; this is a central pathway in the network (Ferris et al., 1974; Ferris et al., 1978; Matthews & Moser, 1966; Miyakawa, Cleaves & Miller, 2002).

reactions.name: Cyanoacetylene route to aspartic acid from HCN and ammonia

conditions.other: pH ~9, room temperature

reactions.mechanism: Cyanoacetylene reacts with cyanide and ammonia in aqueous alkaline solution through C3+C1 nitrile coupling chemistry to generate aspartate among the products.
reactions.reasoning: This route is explicitly demonstrated as a distinct non-Strecker synthesis of aspartate, asparagine, and cytosine at around pH 9 and room temperature; its atmospheric plausibility is debated, but the chemistry itself is established (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Shapiro, 1999).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge atmospheric synthesis delivering HCN and aspartate precursors

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge, bulk aqueous trap near ambient
conditions.pressure: reduced-pressure gas mixtures; classic Miller conditions 100-200 torr components
conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge with oxidation-inhibiting mineral surfaces
conditions.other: week-scale spark discharge; H2S-rich volcanic variants and pyrite/FeSO4 oxidation inhibitors increase amino acid yields

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in reduced or volcanic gas mixtures generates nitriles and amino acid precursors; products collected in water include confirmed aspartate in modern reanalysis.
reactions.reasoning: Modern reanalysis confirmed D,L-aspartate in Miller-type archived samples, and H2S-rich volcanic apparatus increased amino acid production; pyrite and related oxidation inhibitors improve yields in neutral-atmosphere variants, supporting inclusion of mineralized surface discharge chemistry (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1957; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011; Cleaves et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Photochemical synthesis of aspartic acid from reduced atmospheric mixtures

conditions.catalyst: UV irradiation with photocatalytic mineral surfaces
conditions.other: strong UV flux; reduced gas/water mixtures

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation of reduced gas mixtures in water generates nitriles and oxygenated organics that lead to amino acids including aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: Photochemical amino acid formation including aspartate is directly reported from UV-irradiated CH4/C2H6/NH3/H2O/H2S mixtures and related interstellar-ice analogs; TiO2 is included as a plausible surface photocatalyst consistent with surface-mineral requirements (Sagan & Khare, 1971; Muñoz Caro et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Fox-Windsor thermal formaldehyde-ammonia route to aspartate-containing amino acid mixture

conditions.other: heated formaldehyde-ammonia mixtures in evaporative settings

reactions.mechanism: Thermal condensation and rearrangement chemistry in concentrated carbonyl-ammonia mixtures yields amino acid products including aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: Aspartate has been reported from heated formaldehyde and ammonia mixtures; this route is less mechanistically resolved than HCN oligomer or OAA-based chemistry but is part of the literature-supported aspartate inventory and is included as a lower-confidence auxiliary pathway (Fox & Windsor, 1970).

reactions.name: HCN hydration to formamide under UV-active surface conditions

conditions.other: UV irradiation in surface pools

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide hydration and photochemical processing generate formamide, which serves as a concentrated C-N feedstock for downstream prebiotic chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide-centered photochemistry is a standard surface prebiotic module and provides a literature-consistent way to connect HCN-rich chemistry to the Fox-Windsor-type thermal route without inventing unsupported direct feedstocks; TiO2 is known to promote formamide photochemistry to biologically relevant products (Sutherland, 2016 review context; Saladino et al. formamide literature; TiO2 relevance noted in prebiotic photochemistry).

reactions.name: ZnS-analog photocatalytic reduction of oxaloacetate to malate

conditions.other: photochemical reducing conditions; network uses sulfide/TiO2 surface analogs to represent documented semiconductor photocatalysis

reactions.mechanism: Photocatalytic reduction converts oxaloacetate into malate in the documented OAA/malate/fumarate branch of non-enzymatic rTCA analog chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature specifically identifies photocatalytic reduction of oxaloacetate to malate on ZnS surfaces as part of a plausible route to fumarate; this step is retained to fix the weakness of unsupported direct OAA-to-fumarate conversion and is represented here with required surface mineral agents as a constrained proxy for semiconductor photocatalysis (Guzman & Martin, 2008; Zhou & Guzman, 2016).

reactions.name: Dehydration of malate to fumarate

conditions.other: thermal dehydration or drying-interface conversion

reactions.mechanism: Malate loses water to generate fumarate, establishing the unsaturated dicarboxylate branch relevant to aspartate chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: This step follows the literature-supported malate-to-fumarate sequence used in non-enzymatic rTCA analog discussion and avoids the previously criticized unsupported direct OAA-to-fumarate shortcut (Guzman & Martin, 2008; Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2020).

reactions.name: Non-enzymatic Michael addition of ammonia to fumarate giving aspartate

conditions.other: alkaline conditions with high NH3 concentration; low-yield thermodynamically feasible reaction

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia adds across the activated alkene of fumarate to produce aspartate through the reverse of the biological aspartase transformation.
reactions.reasoning: This route is documented as thermodynamically feasible but low-yield without enzymatic catalysis; it is therefore included as a weak but literature-supported terminal path rather than overclaimed as a major source (Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2020; Harrison et al., 2023 background discussion).

reactions.name: Wet-dry clay-assisted peptide formation from glycine and aspartate

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling at about 85 +/- 5 C

reactions.mechanism: Clay interlayers concentrate amino acids during dehydration and facilitate peptide bond formation to glycyl-aspartic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Aspartate is especially relevant as a nodal metabolite only if it can enter further chemistry; montmorillonite-assisted formation of glycyl-aspartic acid is experimentally documented under prebiotic wet-dry cycling conditions (Lahav et al., 1978; Ferris & Ertem, 1992 context; Kalra/Pant et al.).

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal Fe0-hydroxylamine route
pathways.description: Direct hydrothermal production of aspartate within an Fe0-driven CO2 fixation network using hydroxylamine as nitrogen source.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic OAA to Cu-pyridoxamine transamination
pathways.description: CO2 fixation produces glyoxylate and pyruvate, which feed transient oxaloacetate that is rapidly transaminated to aspartate.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic OAA to Bucherer-Bergs aspartate
pathways.description: Vent-generated keto-acid network feeds oxaloacetate, which enters the Bucherer-Bergs route to N-carbamoyl aspartate and then hydrolyzes to aspartate.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Surface-concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN/higher polymers that hydrolyze to aspartate as a major product.

pathways.name: Spark discharge to HCN oligomer hydrolysis
pathways.description: Atmospheric spark discharge generates HCN, which then oligomerizes and hydrolyzes to aspartate.

pathways.name: Spark discharge direct aspartate route
pathways.description: Electrical discharge in volcanic/reduced gas mixtures produces a dilute but direct aspartate output captured in aqueous reservoirs.

pathways.name: Cyanoacetylene alkaline pool route
pathways.description: Cyanoacetylene reacts with HCN and ammonia at pH about 9 in surface waters to yield aspartate directly.

pathways.name: Photochemical atmospheric route
pathways.description: UV irradiation of reduced gas mixtures generates aspartate directly and also feeds HCN-rich chemistry.

pathways.name: Photochemical fumarate-ammonia route
pathways.description: Transient oxaloacetate is photoreduced to malate, dehydrated to fumarate, then aminated under alkaline NH3-rich conditions to give aspartate.

pathways.name: Formamide-thermal auxiliary route
pathways.description: HCN is converted to formamide in UV-active pools, and concentrated heated formamide/ammonia chemistry yields aspartate in a surface thermal setting.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the stronger single-step routes because the literature specifically supports Fe0-mediated CO2 reduction with hydroxylamine producing aspartate among the product set under moderate hydrothermal conditions. The main weakness is that the reaction is represented as an overly compressed one-step transformation directly from CO2 and NH2OH to aspartate, pyruvate, and acetate without intermediate accounting, so the mechanistic realism is lower than the literature-inspired concept. Still, it aligns reasonably well with Varma/Muchowska/Moran-style chemistry.
suggestions: Expand the reaction into intermediate Fe0-mediated carbon fixation steps leading through glyoxylate/pyruvate or oxaloacetate-like intermediates
Specify moderate hydrothermal conditions closer to 30-100C and elevated pressure rather than generic hydrothermal framing
Clarify that aspartate is part of a product mixture with likely low yield

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway captures an important literature theme: hydrothermal protometabolic carbon fixation feeding oxaloacetate formation, then Cu2+/pyridoxamine transamination to aspartate. The final transamination step is well aligned with Harrison et al. 2023. However, the pyruvate + glyoxylate to oxaloacetate step is too simplified and not the experimentally established direct route; literature more often invokes 4-hydroxy-2-ketoglutarate-type intermediates and oxidative processing. The pathway is conceptually strong but chemically compressed at the oxaloacetate-forming step.
suggestions: Replace the direct pyruvate + glyoxylate -> oxaloacetate step with a better-supported sequence via 4-hydroxy-2-ketoglutarate or related intermediates
Include explicit acknowledgement of oxaloacetate instability and rapid consumption conditions
State Cu2+ catalyst concentration, pH 6-10, and 25-70C conditions to better match the literature

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the best pathways in the network because it prioritizes the Bucherer-Bergs route via oxaloacetate, N-carbamoyl aspartate, and the dihydroorotate branch, which closely matches Pulletikurti et al. 2022. The major limitation again lies upstream in the oversimplified generation of oxaloacetate from pyruvate and glyoxylate. Once oxaloacetate is available, the downstream chemistry is well chosen and literature-aligned.
suggestions: Improve the prebiotic plausibility of oxaloacetate formation by adding intermediate steps and appropriate oxidants or redox context
Specify cyanide and ammonium bicarbonate explicitly rather than generic HCN + NH3 to better reflect Bucherer-Bergs conditions
Include room temperature to mild heating and neutral to slightly basic pH in conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest pathway in the network and the best aligned with the literature. HCN oligomerization to DAMN/higher polymers followed by hydrolysis yielding aspartate and glycine is directly supported by Ferris et al., Matthews and Moser, and eutectic concentration studies. The pathway reflects the literature conclusion that HCN oligomer hydrolysis is the most productive prebiotic route specifically for aspartate.
suggestions: Add the requirement for sufficiently high HCN concentration, ideally >10^-2 M and alkaline pH around 9.2
Mention acid hydrolysis conditions explicitly because amino acid release depends on hydrolysis of the oligomeric products
Optionally include the aspartate-related hydantoin intermediate for stronger mechanistic fidelity

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is acceptable overall because the downstream HCN oligomer chemistry is strong. The weak point is the atmospheric spark-discharge reaction, which is depicted as directly producing substantial aspartate plus HCN and cyanoacetylene from a CH4/NH3/H2S/CO2 mixture. Literature supports spark discharge generation of amino acids and HCN, but aspartate yields are low, atmosphere dependence is important, and cyanoacetylene formation is suppressed by NH3 or H2S. So the first step is overgeneralized and partly inconsistent with the detailed research findings.
suggestions: Separate atmospheric generation of HCN from aqueous conversion to amino acids
Reduce confidence in cyanoacetylene co-production under NH3/H2S-containing discharge mixtures
Reflect that confirmed aspartate in spark experiments is low-yield rather than a major direct product

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pathway is too compressed and overstates direct aspartate production from spark-discharge atmospheric chemistry. Literature confirms low-yield aspartate in some archived Miller samples and volcanic/H2S variants, but not as a robust direct single-step route in the way shown here. Co-generation of cyanoacetylene under these exact gas mixtures is especially questionable.
suggestions: Represent spark discharge as producing precursor mixtures rather than direct target formation
Use HCN, formaldehyde, nitriles, and simple amino acid mixtures as outputs instead of direct aspartate emphasis
Tie any aspartate formation to subsequent aqueous chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is well supported by Sanchez, Ferris, and Orgel-type chemistry. Cyanoacetylene reacting with HCN and ammonia in aqueous alkaline solution to yield aspartate is a legitimate distinct route. The main caveat is environmental availability: cyanoacetylene abundance is disputed under realistic atmospheres, and the network does not clearly explain how enough cyanoacetylene accumulates in the chosen surface setting.
suggestions: Strengthen the environmental story for cyanoacetylene supply or treat it as episodic/localized
Specify aqueous pH around 9 and room temperature conditions
Note that this route also connects to cytosine chemistry, which could improve network richness

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A photochemical atmospheric route to aspartate is literature-supported in broad terms by Sagan and Khare, but the network again compresses a complex product-distribution experiment into a direct single-step synthesis of aspartate and HCN. That makes the chemistry less specific and less informative than it could be.
suggestions: Model photochemistry as generating a product mixture including amino acids rather than a direct named conversion
Specify UV irradiation conditions and gas composition in more detail
Treat aspartate as minor product unless yield evidence is provided

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway has good conceptual value because fumarate is a relevant node linking oxaloacetate chemistry to aspartate. However, the details are weak. The literature emphasizes ZnS photocatalysis for oxaloacetate to malate-related chemistry, not the ZnS-analog phrasing used here, and the non-enzymatic Michael addition of ammonia to fumarate is known to be very low yielding without enzymes. So the pathway is chemically possible but poor as a productive target route.
suggestions: Use explicit ZnS rather than a vague ZnS-analog photocatalyst if supported by the source literature
Downgrade the fumarate + ammonia step to a low-efficiency auxiliary route
Include high ammonia concentration and alkaline conditions if retaining the Michael addition step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN hydration to formamide is reasonable, and Fox-Windsor style heated formaldehyde/ammonia chemistry does have literature support for producing aspartate among amino acid mixtures. But the network uses formamide as the precursor for the Fox-Windsor route rather than formaldehyde, which is a notable mismatch. That weakens literature alignment and mechanistic accuracy.
suggestions: Replace formamide with formaldehyde if the intention is to represent the Fox-Windsor pathway
Clarify whether formamide is acting only as a proxy feedstock to formaldehyde-derived products
Describe the route as giving an amino acid mixture rather than a focused aspartate synthesis

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong: it has ample molecule and reaction nodes, multiple hubs, several convergence points, and no obvious isolated subgraphs. It also does a good job capturing the major literature-supported conceptual routes to aspartate, especially HCN oligomer hydrolysis, oxaloacetate-centered Bucherer-Bergs chemistry, Cu2+/pyridoxamine transamination, and the oxaloacetate decarboxylation bottleneck to pyruvate/alanine. Environmental interconnection is generally good, with hydrothermal carbon fixation feeding surface and biochemical routes. However, the overall confidence is held back by several chemically compressed or overstated reactions. The most serious issues are: direct pyruvate + glyoxylate -> oxaloacetate without the better-supported intermediate sequence; atmospheric/spark and photochemical reactions presented as unrealistically direct aspartate-producing steps; a fumarate-to-aspartate route that is plausible but weakly productive; and a Fox-Windsor representation that uses formamide instead of formaldehyde. Mineral-agent compliance is only partial relative to the original network-generation standard: hydrothermal and surface reactions are named with environments, but many listed reactions as evaluated here do not explicitly show mineral catalysts in the reaction summaries, and some surface reactions rely on non-required or vaguely specified agents. Literature alignment is mixed but respectable: the strongest pathways are well aligned, while several auxiliary pathways overinterpret older broad product-mixture studies. Overall, this is a good but not approval-level network.
